coredata.dc:title: Preparation of bright blue fluorescent carbon dots and their application in highly sensitive chlorogenic acid detection
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry Chinese Academy of Sciences.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry Chinese Academy of Sciences.
coredata.dc:creator: PAN, Cong-Jie
DENG, Xin-Xin
LU, Mei-Cheng
QIN, Xue-Zhen
coredata.dc:description: In this work, bright blue fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized through a hydrothermal method by using l-phenylephrine as carbon source. Under exciting at 363 nm, the prepared CDs emitted bright blue fluorescence at 457 nm. The fabricated CDs showed outstanding salt tolerance, temperature stability and good resistance to photobleaching. Interestingly, the fluorescence of CDs could be distinctly quenched by chlorogenic acid (CGA). By using CDs as fluorescent probe, a novel fluorescence method for highly sensitive detection of CGA was established with a wide linear range of 0.1–220 µM and a limit of detection (LOD) as low as 33 nM. The response mechanism was also studied. The method has been successfully applied for the detection of CGA content in three samples of honeysuckle, coffee beans and Eucommia ulmoides leaves with satisfactory recoveries.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescence
Chlorogenic acid
Internal filtration effect

Response mechanism of CDs to CGA

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM and (b) the corresponding size distribution histogram of CDs.

UV–Vis absorption spectra (blue curve), fluorescence excitation spectra (black curve) and emission spectra (red curve) of CDs. The inset was the CDs solution under natural light (left) and 365 nm UV light (right).

(a) Normalized FL intensity of CDs in PBS buffer solution of different pH values; (b) Normalized FL intensity of CDs at different temperatures; (c) Normalized FL intensity of CDs in NaCl solution with different concentrations; (d) FL intensity of CDs exposed to 363 nm light source for 60 min. The error bar is the standard deviation of three measurements.

(a) Normalized FL intensity of CDs (green bar) and the subsequent addition of 60 µM CGA (orange bar) in PBS buffer solution at different pH values; (b) Time-dependent FL intensity of CDs solution after addition of 60 µM CGA.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs after addition of different concentrations of CGA. The inset was the CDs solution without (left) and with CGA (right) under 365 nm UV light; (b) linear relationship between log(F 0/F) of CDs and the concentration of CGA.

(a) Normalized FL intensity of CDs solution after addition of 200 µM inorganic ions (green bar) and the subsequent addition of 60 µM CGA (orange bar); (b) normalized FL intensity of CDs solution after addition of 200 µM organic substances (green bar) and the subsequent addition of 60 µM CGA (orange bar).

(a) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CDs, CGA, and the experimental and theoretical UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the mixture of CDs and CGA. (b) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CGA (blue curve), the fluorescence excitation (black curve) and emission (red curve) spectrum of CDs. (c) Fluorescence decay curves of CDs and the mixture solution of CDs with different concentrations of CGA. (d) Parameters related to Eq. (1). s: The thickness of the excitation beam, s = 0.50 cm; d: The thickness of the cuvette, d = 1.00 cm; g: The distance between the edge of the excitation beam and the edge of the cuvette, g = 0.25 cm.
Schematic diagram of the synthesis process of CDs and their application for CGA detection.
Comparison of different fluorescence methods for the detection of CGA.
Results of the detection of CGA in real samples.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Bright blue fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized through a simple one-pot hydrothermal method by using l-phenylephrine as carbon source. By utilizing the prepared CDs as probe, a novel fluorescence method was constructed for highly sensitive detection of chlorogenic acid (CGA) based on internal filtration effect (IFE). The established method possessed the advantages of simple operation, rapid response, high sensitivity, wide linear range and low LOD.

head.ce:title: Preparation of bright blue fluorescent carbon dots and their application in highly sensitive chlorogenic acid detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bHenan Engineering Research Center of Modern Chinese Medicine Research, Development and Application, Zhengzhou 450046, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, bright blue fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized through a hydrothermal method by using l-phenylephrine as carbon source. Under exciting at 363 nm, the prepared CDs emitted bright blue fluorescence at 457 nm. The fabricated CDs showed outstanding salt tolerance, temperature stability and good resistance to photobleaching. Interestingly, the fluorescence of CDs could be distinctly quenched by chlorogenic acid (CGA). By using CDs as fluorescent probe, a novel fluorescence method for highly sensitive detection of CGA was established with a wide linear range of 0.1–220 µM and a limit of detection (LOD) as low as 33 nM. The response mechanism was also studied. The method has been successfully applied for the detection of CGA content in three samples of honeysuckle, coffee beans and Eucommia ulmoides leaves with satisfactory recoveries.

Bright blue fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized through a simple onepot hydrothermal method by using l-phenylephrine as carbon source. By utilizing the prepared CDs as probe, a novel fluorescence method was constructed for highly sensitive detection of chlorogenic acid (CGA) based on internal filtration effect (IFE). The established method possessed the advantages of simple operation, rapid response, high sensitivity, wide linear range and low LOD.
Image, graphical abstract

ce:section.ce:para: Chlorogenic acid (CGA) is a kind of phenylpropyl compound produced by caffeic acid and quinidine through shikimic acid pathway during aerobic respiration of plants [1]. CGA is widely found in various plants, such as sunflower, coffee and cacao tree [2]. Significantly, it is also a bioactive ingredient of traditional Chinese herbs such as honeysuckle and Eucommia ulmoides leaves [3,4]. As an important bioactive compound, CGA has the functions of antibacterial, antiviral, anti-tumor, lowering blood pressure, lowering blood lipids and stimulating the central nervous system, which can effectively remove free radicals in the body, maintain normal function of body cells, prevent tumor mutation and delay aging [5,6]. Due to the above excellent properties, CGA has been widely used in pharmaceutical, food and daily chemical industry in recent years. On account of this, it is important to establish a highly sensitive method for effective detection of CGA.

To date, the methods for the determination of CGA mainly include chemiluminescence [7], high performance liquid chromatography [8], thin-layer scanning [9], electrochemical [10] and fluorescence method [11,12]. Among them, fluorescence method is considered to be an effective method in recent years due to its advantages of high sensitivity, good selectivity, simple and fast operation, and low cost. For example, Liu et al. synthesized cyan fluorescent silicon quantum dots by one-pot hydrothermal method and utilized them as fluorescent probe for CGA detection [11]. Although some fluorescence methods have been reported for CGA detection, it is still of great significance to develop a reliable, simple and sensitive fluorescence method for CGA detection.

Carbon dots (CDs), a type of luminescent carbon-nanomaterial which can be easily synthesized, have received much attention owing to their advantages of outstanding fluorescence properties, favorable stability, good water solubility, excellent biocompatibility and facile preparation [13,14]. CDs with high quantum yield usually can be synthesized by inexpensive carbon source through the typical hydrothermal method. The excellent properties and simple synthesis procedure of CDs allows them to be widely used as fluorescent probes in the field of fluorescence detection in recent years. So far, CDs have been applied for the detection of ions [15,16], biomolecules [17], pH [18], and temperature [19], etc. Significantly, a few literatures about CDs as fluorescent probes in the detection of CGA have also been reported recently. For example, Yang et al. utilized malic acid and urea as precursors to synthesize CDs by a hydrothermal method for detecting CGA with the linear range of 0.15–60 µM [12]. Liu et al. fabricated nitrogen and sulfur co-doped CDs as fluorescence probe for effective detection of CGA with the linear range of 0.33–29.70 µg mL−1 [20]. The above results indicated that CDs as fluorescence probe showed good responses to CGA detection. Nevertheless, the reported fluorescence methods for CGA detection still have some shortcomings such as relatively narrow linear range and sensitivity to be improved. Developing CDs with simple preparation process and outstanding properties and utilizing them for highly sensitive detection of CGA are desirable.

In this work, by using l-phenylephrine as carbon source, bright blue fluorescent CDs were synthesized through a simple one-pot hydrothermal method (Scheme 1 ). The as-prepared CDs exhibited robust stability and outstanding optical properties. It was found that the fluorescence of CDs could be significantly quenched by CGA. Based on the above phenomenon, a novel fluorescence method for detection of CGA was established by using the prepared CDs as probe (Scheme 1). The method has superior advantages of simple synthesis process, rapid response, wide linear range and high sensitivity. The proposed method was applied to detect CGA in the samples of honeysuckle, coffee beans and Eucommia ulmoides leaves. The possible sensing mechanism of CDs to CGA was also discussed.

In this experiment, a one-step hydrothermal method was used to synthesize CDs. The synthesis steps were as follows: Firstly, 10.0 mg of l-phenylephrine was added to 5 mL of deionized water under stirring at room temperature for 15 min. Then, the mixture solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined reactor. The reaction was carried out at 200 °C for 4 h. After that, the obtained CDs solution was cooled to room temperature and then centrifuged for 15 min (15,000 min−1). Finally, the supernatant was purified with dialysis bag (1000 Da, molecular weight cut-off). The obtained CDs solution was stored in a refrigerator at 4 ℃ for later use.

Firstly, CGA was dissolved in deionized water to prepare 10 mM CGA stock solution. Then, the stock solution was further diluted with PBS buffer solution (pH 9.0, 10 mM) to prepare a series of CGA solutions with different concentrations. 25 µL of CDs solution was mixed with different volume of CGA solution. Then, the above solutions were diluted with PBS solution (the total volume was 2 mL), respectively. After incubation for 1 min, the fluorescence emission spectra of the above solutions were measured at the excitation wavelength of 363 nm. Each measurement was repeated three times. The working curve was drawn using CGA concentration and Log (F 0/F) value of CDs (F 0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity (FL intensity) of CDs in the absence and presence of CGA, respectively).

The selectivity experiment was carried out by adding potential interfering substances instead of CGA. The anti-interference experiment was also carried out by adding CGA and potential interfering substances simultaneously under the same conditions. In the selectivity and anti-interference experiments, the concentration of CGA was 60 µM, and the concentration of interfering substance was 200 µM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of CGA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the applicability of the established method, honeysuckle, coffee beans and Eucommia ulmoides leaves were selected as real samples. The pretreatment process of the above three samples was seen in supporting information. Under optimal conditions, the contents of CGA in the above samples were detected through the established fluorescence method. The detection process was as follows: 15 , 25 and 40 µL of honeysuckle, coffee bean and Eucommia ulmoides leave stock solution were mixed with 25 µL CDs, respectively. Then, the mixed solution were diluted by PBS (pH 9.0, 10 mM) with the final volume of 2.0 mL. After 1 min incubation, the FL intensity of the above mixture solution at 457 nm was measured. The original contents of CGA in the samples were calculated by using the established working curve. Then, known amounts of CGA (10, 50 and 100 µM) were spiked into the samples for recovery experiment.

In order to obtain CDs with excellent fluorescence properties, the dosage of carbon source l-phenylephrine, reaction time and reaction temperature were optimized, respectively. As shown in Fig. S1a, with the increase of the amount of l-phenylephrine, the FL intensity of CDs gradually increased and then decreased. When the dosage of l-phenylephrine was 10 mg, the FL intensity of CDs was the strongest. As seen in Fig. S1b, the FL intensity of CDs was the highest when the reaction time was 4 h. As illustrated in Fig. S1c, the FL intensity of CDs was significantly enhanced with the increase of temperature. Considering FL intensity and experimental safety, the reaction temperature was fixed at 200 °C. According to the above experimental results, the optimal conditions for the synthesis of CDs were l-phenylephrine 10 mg, reaction time 4 h and reaction temperature 200 °C, respectively.

Firstly, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was used to characterize the morphology of CDs. As shown in Fig. 1 a, CDs were quasi-spherical and well dispersed. The particle size of CDs ranged from 1.1 to 2.3 nm, with an average particle size of 1.61 nm (Fig. 1b). Then, the structure of CDs was characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR). As seen in Fig. S2, the 3446 cm−1 absorption band was attributed to the stretching vibration of NH [21]. The 3369 cm−1 absorption band was the stretching vibration of OH [22]. The absorption band at 3124 cm−1 was the stretching vibration of CH [23]. The absorption band at 1699 cm−1 was the stretching vibration of C=O [24]. 1266 cm−1 was the CN stretching vibration absorption band [23]. The peak at 1063 and 1129 cm−1 could be attributed to CO stretching vibration [23]. 849 cm−1 was the absorption band of NH wagging vibration [25]. The FT-IR results suggested that CDs possessed abundant hydroxyl and amino functional groups, indicating the good water dispersion of CDs. Furthermore, the optical properties of CDs were analyzed by UV–Vis absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra. As seen in Fig. 2 , typical absorption bands centered at 220 and 350 nm could be attributed to π-π* transition of C=C bond and n-π* transition of C=O bond, respectively [24]. When the excitation wavelength was 363 nm, the FL intensity of CDs reached the highest at 457 nm. Fig. S3 was the fluorescence emission spectra of CDs at different excitation wavelengths ranged from 320 to 400 nm. As shown in Fig. S3, the fluorescence emission peak was not shifted with the change of excitation wavelength, suggesting that the fluorescence emission behavior was independent [26]. The relative fluorescence quantum yield of CDs was calculated to be 4.17% (Fig. S4).

The pH stability, salt resistance stability, thermal stability and photobleaching resistance of CDs were investigated in the following. As shown in Fig. 3 a, with the increase of pH, the FL intensity of CDs increased firstly and then tended to be stable in the pH range of 9.0–12.0. As shown in Fig. 3b, the FL intensity of CDs remained relatively stable with the increase of temperature, indicating that CDs exhibited outstanding temperature stability. It could be seen from Fig. 3c that the FL intensity of CDs changed slightly with the increase of NaCl concentration, indicating that CDs had good salt-resistant stability. As shown in Fig. 3d, the FL intensity of CDs did not change significantly with the continuous irradiation by light source (λ ex= 363 nm) for 60 min, indicating strong photobleaching resistance of CDs. The above results illustrated that the synthesized CDs have robust stability.

In order to obtain good sensitivity, the pH of PBS buffer solution and response time were investigated. As shown in Fig. 4 a, CGA showed the highest fluorescence quenching efficiency for CDs at pH 9.0. Therefore, pH 9.0 was selected as the optimal pH value in the following experiments. As shown in Fig. 4b, when CDs and CGA were evenly mixed, the FL intensity of CDs could be quenched significantly within 30 s. Considering the convenience of time control, 1 min was selected as the optimum response time.

In order to better apply the method to the analysis of real samples, the selectivity and anti-interference ability of the proposed method were studied in the following. The effects of common interfering ions (Ni2+, Na+, K+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Ba2+, Cl−, NO3 −, CO3 2− and HCO3 −) and potential organic interfering substances (glutathione, creatine, sucrose, arginine, glycine, serine and glucose) on the system were investigated. As seen in Fig. 6 , the FL intensity of CDs did not change significantly when 200 µM interference substance was added to CDs solution. Only when CGA was added, the FL intensity of CDs solution decreased significantly. This indicated that CDs have good selectivity for CGA detection. Moreover, the interference substances and CGA were added to CDs solution simultaneously to further study the anti-interference ability of the method. As shown in Fig. 6, there was no significant difference between the FL intensity of CDs solution with only CGA and the FL intensity of CDs solution with CGA and interfering substances simultaneously, which suggested that CDs have good anti-interference ability. The results above provided a basis for the detection of CGA in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of CGA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the practicability of the established method, it was applied to the detection of CGA contents in the samples of honeysuckle, coffee beans and Eucommia ulmoides leaves. After calculation, the contents of CGA in the above three samples were 51.97, 74.75 and 42.30 mg g−1, respectively. Additionally, the recoveries of the three samples were measured by adding different concentrations of CGA. As can be seen from Table 2 , the average recoveries ranged from 91.51% to 105.0% with relative standard deviations (RSDs) no more than 4.57%. The above results indicated that the established method can be used for the determination of CGA contents in real samples with good accuracy and precision.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Response mechanism of CDs to CGA
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to investigate the response mechanism of CDs to CGA, a series of experiments were carried out. Firstly, the UV–Vis absorption spectra of CDs, CGA, and the mixture of CDs and CGA were studied. As seen in Fig. 7 a, the experimental UV–Vis absorption spectra of the mixture of CDs and CGA were almost completely overlapped with the theoretical curve, indicating that no new compound was formed between them. From Fig. 7b, it can be seen that the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CGA overlapped with the fluorescence excitation spectrum of CDs to a large extent. Based on the above results, we speculated that the quenching mechanism may be caused by the internal filtration effect (IFE) [28]. In the following, in order to verify the IFE mechanism, the fluorescence lifetimes of CDs solution with different concentration of CGA were investigated firstly. As shown in Fig. 7c and Table S1, the fluorescence lifetime of CDs did not change significantly after the addition of different concentrations of CGA, indicating that the quenching mechanism was due to IFE. Next, the IFE quenching mechanism was further verified by calculating the IFE proportion by Eq.1 [29].
(1)
F
cor

2.3 d
A

− d
A

g
A

2.3 s
A

1 − 1
0
− s
A
em

The correction factor (CF) values were calculated through Eq. (1) to correct the detected FL intensity. To ensure the reliability of calculation, the CF value should not more than 3 [30]. The parameters related to Eq. (1) were illustrated in Fig. 7d. After calculation, the proportion of IFE was up to 95.38% (Table S2). In summary, the response mechanism of CDs to CGA was mainly due to IFE.

Then, the binding interaction between CDs and CGA was further studied. According to the structures and functional groups of CDs and CGA, electrostatic interaction or hydrogen-bond interaction might exist between them. As shown in Fig. S5, the zeta potentials of CDs, CGA, and the mixture of CDs and CGA solution were −10.4, −14.0, and −11.1 mV, respectively. This showed that no electrostatic interaction existed between the CDs and CGA. Hence, we speculated that the interaction between CDs and CGA might be contributed to hydrogen bonds [31].

In conclusion, bright blue fluorescent CDs were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method by using l-phenylephrine as carbon source. The synthesized CDs exhibited outstanding optical properties and robust stability. Based on IFE mechanism, a novel fluorescence method has been successfully established by utilizing CDs as fluorescent probe for detection of CGA with good accuracy and precision. The linear range was 0.1–220 µM with an LOD of 33 nM. The method exhibited the advantages of simple operation, rapid response, high sensitivity and wide linear range. The method has been applied to detect the contents of CGA in the samples of honeysuckle, coffee beans and Eucommia ulmoides leaves with satisfactory recoveries.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing interests.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful for financial support from the Zhongjing Scholars Research Funding of Henan University of Chinese Medicine (No. 00104311–2023–13).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.cjac.2023.100334.
